title: Do not turn every group into a card
principle: Use cards only for bounded, reusable, selectable, or independently actionable objects; group ordinary content with rhythm and structure.
rationale: Card saturation flattens hierarchy, adds nested chrome, wastes space, and makes unrelated and related regions look equally important.
applies_when: A composition contains repeated bordered or elevated rectangles.
exceptions: Cards are appropriate for discrete products, files, people, records, or draggable/selectable objects.
implementation: Remove borders and backgrounds from candidate groups, then restore only boundaries that clarify object identity or interaction.
Use headings, alignment, spacing, dividers, and lists for document structure.
verification: Every remaining card has a sentence explaining its independent object or interaction boundary.
Hierarchy remains clear without nested cards.
accessibility: Use semantic regions, lists, headings, and groups; card styling alone does not create a programmatic relationship.
responsive: Removing unnecessary containers prevents excessive inset and narrow text columns on small screens.
performance: Fewer wrapper elements and effects reduce layout and paint complexity.
browser_notes: No special browser considerations beyond standard layout testing.

anti_pattern.why_ai_overuses: Cards are a high-frequency training example, create obvious visual segmentation from sparse prompts, and let generators avoid reasoning about semantic proximity.
detection: More than half of top-level sections have their own rounded border or shadow.
Cards contain cards without independent interaction boundaries.
Removing card chrome reveals a simple document outline.
appropriate_contexts: Discrete reusable objects with independent actions, selection, drag behavior, or destination.
inappropriate_contexts: Sequential prose, form sections, simple metadata, navigation groups, and arbitrary landing-page sections.
verification: Annotate the object boundary for every card; remove any card without one.
Review the page in grayscale and without shadows.

title: Do not use ambient gradients as default personality
principle: Use gradients when they encode a brand, spatial, data, or lighting idea; do not scatter purple-blue auroras behind generic content to simulate originality.
rationale: Unmotivated gradients reduce contrast predictability, date quickly, and make distinct products converge on the same synthetic aesthetic.
applies_when: A design contains blurred color blobs, gradient text, glow fields, or multi-hue CTA fills.
exceptions: A tested brand gradient, continuous data scale, natural lighting treatment, or restrained depth cue may be appropriate.
implementation: Name the gradient's semantic or brand role and restrict it to that role.
Prefer authored type, composition, image direction, or color blocking for identity.
verification: Turn gradients off; the design still has hierarchy and character.
Text and controls pass contrast across every point of the background.
accessibility: Test minimum contrast over the full gradient and avoid gradient-clipped body text.
responsive: Crop and focal behavior do not place text over lower-contrast regions.
performance: Avoid large animated filters and blurred layers that increase paint cost.
browser_notes: Test color interpolation, clipping, and banding on supported engines and displays.

anti_pattern.why_ai_overuses: A gradient produces immediate screenshot-level visual interest from a single CSS declaration and appears frequently in generated landing-page examples.
detection: Decorative gradients appear in multiple unrelated sections.
Purple, cyan, or pink glows have no connection to brand tokens.
Gradient text is used for ordinary headings.
appropriate_contexts: Established brand expression, data scales, bounded illustration lighting, or a single intentional focal moment.
inappropriate_contexts: Default hero backgrounds, every primary button, generic cards, and dense application surfaces.
verification: Document the gradient role and its maximum occurrences.
Audit contrast at sampled stops and remove any gradient without a named role.

title: Do not make every control and label a pill
principle: Reserve fully rounded capsules for compact bounded objects or a deliberate system signature; use geometry to distinguish roles rather than homogenize them.
rationale: Pill saturation makes buttons, filters, badges, tags, and fields visually interchangeable and consumes horizontal space.
applies_when: Multiple component categories share maximum border radius.
exceptions: Tags, tokens, segmented choices, status chips, and a signature primary action may legitimately use capsules.
implementation: Define radius tokens by semantic component family.
Use text, spacing, underline, border, or shape changes to preserve role distinction.
verification: A grayscale component inventory still distinguishes action, status, input, and navigation roles.
Long localized labels do not create awkward capsules.
accessibility: Shape is never the only indicator of role or state.
responsive: Pills wrap intentionally and do not create horizontal scrollers for primary actions.
browser_notes: Native form affordances remain recognizable after radius styling.

anti_pattern.why_ai_overuses: Maximum radius is an easy global token that creates superficial consistency and a friendly contemporary look without component-role analysis.
detection: Buttons, inputs, badges, navigation, and cards all have near-identical capsule silhouettes.
More than three semantic component families use full rounding.
Long labels look like stretched lozenges.
appropriate_contexts: Tags, tokens, compact filters, segmented controls, and a consciously branded action family.
inappropriate_contexts: Long form fields, all navigation, large cards, dense tables, and every button hierarchy.
verification: Inventory components by semantic role and radius token.
Require a reason when unrelated families share the capsule token.

title: Do not force content into a bento grid
principle: Use asymmetric modular grids only when module size expresses priority or content type; do not use a mosaic as a substitute for information architecture.
rationale: Bento grids often assign arbitrary size, fragment narrative reading, and collapse into an undifferentiated card stack on small screens.
applies_when: A page uses mixed-size rounded tiles to present unrelated features or proof.
exceptions: A dashboard or portfolio with genuinely modular, differently weighted items may benefit from a spatial overview.
implementation: Rank modules and define why each span differs.
Choose a sequence, comparison, table, list, or editorial layout when those better match relationships.
verification: Tile size correlates with decision value or content need.
The narrow-screen sequence preserves the intended hierarchy.
accessibility: DOM order follows meaning, not mosaic coordinates, and visual size is not the only priority cue.
responsive: Test intermediate widths where spanning modules can create holes or tiny columns.
performance: Avoid loading decorative media merely to fill grid cells.
browser_notes: Grid fallback and source order remain coherent.

anti_pattern.why_ai_overuses: Bento compositions are visually legible in generated screenshots, tolerate unrelated placeholder content, and are strongly represented in contemporary inspiration sets.
detection: Tile spans vary without a content or priority explanation.
Every section is a rounded grid cell.
Mobile becomes a long sequence of equal cards with lost hierarchy.
appropriate_contexts: Modular dashboards, portfolios, or feature overviews where area and placement communicate real priority.
inappropriate_contexts: Linear onboarding, narrative marketing, forms, documentation, and arbitrarily grouped feature lists.
alternatives: single dominant feature with supporting proof
verification: Annotate each tile's span rationale.
Convert the grid to a linear outline and confirm the hierarchy survives.

title: Do not spend the first viewport on oversized generic hero copy
principle: Size the opening composition to communicate value, evidence, and next action quickly; use extreme scale only when the message itself earns it.
rationale: A full viewport containing a vague super-sized slogan delays proof, reduces information scent, and becomes especially wasteful on laptops and mobile landscape.
applies_when: A hero uses very large type, large vertical padding, or little content above the fold.
exceptions: A campaign, launch, cultural work, or editorial statement may intentionally use cinematic scale when the words and art direction are distinctive.
implementation: Lead with a specific promise, concise qualifier, relevant proof, and one primary action.
Tune scale to content and viewport rather than forcing min-height: 100vh.
verification: At common laptop and mobile heights, users see useful proof or product context without scrolling through empty space.
The heading says something a competitor could not reuse unchanged.
accessibility: Heading wraps predictably at zoom and does not crowd navigation or controls.
responsive: Use bounded fluid type and content-driven block size; account for dynamic viewport units.
performance: Do not make a decorative hero asset the required LCP when text can communicate first.
browser_notes: Avoid fragile viewport-height assumptions under mobile browser chrome.

anti_pattern.why_ai_overuses: A huge headline and generous whitespace cheaply create a polished screenshot while allowing the generator to avoid product-specific details and proof.
detection: The hero occupies most or all of the initial viewport with fewer than three meaningful elements.
The headline could describe many unrelated products.
Proof or actual product context starts below a large empty band.
appropriate_contexts: Cinematic campaigns, distinctive launches, editorial statements, and products whose primary visual is itself the proof.
inappropriate_contexts: Utility products, conversion pages needing trust, dense applications, and vague early-stage copy.
verification: Review at 768px viewport height and mobile landscape.
Run a competitor substitution test on the headline.

title: Do not stack glass, glow, blur, border, and shadow by default
principle: Choose the minimum surface cue needed to express elevation, boundary, or material; effects must have a physical or semantic model.
rationale: Stacked translucency and glow reduce text contrast, muddy hierarchy, increase paint cost, and create a generic synthetic finish.
applies_when: A surface combines translucency, backdrop blur, gradient, highlight border, glow, and shadow.
exceptions: A tightly art-directed immersive surface may combine effects when contrast, performance, and fallbacks are proven.
implementation: Name the surface relationship and select one primary cue plus at most one supporting cue.
Prefer opaque semantic surfaces for dense reading and controls.
verification: Each effect can be mapped to boundary, elevation, focus, or brand intent.
Removing any effect that has no distinct job improves or preserves comprehension.
accessibility: Text and focus pass against the worst underlying content; transparency is never the only boundary cue.
responsive: Effects do not erase boundaries as background crops change.
performance: Profile backdrop filters, large blurs, and animated shadows on a low-power device.
browser_notes: Provide opaque fallbacks for unsupported backdrop filtering and forced colors.

anti_pattern.why_ai_overuses: Layered CSS effects create instant perceived depth and resemble common concept shots, so generators apply them without a material model or content test.
detection: A single surface uses three or more of blur, transparency, glow, gradient, border, or shadow.
Effects appear on ordinary form and text containers.
Hierarchy collapses when effects are disabled.
appropriate_contexts: A rare immersive or brand-led moment with controlled backgrounds and measured rendering.
inappropriate_contexts: Dense applications, long-form text, forms, repeated cards, and accessibility-critical controls.
verification: List the job of every effect and delete duplicates.
Test on low-power hardware, forced colors, and varied background content.

title: Do not animate every element into view
principle: Animate meaningful state transitions and a few focal moments; render ordinary reading content stably without repeated scroll-triggered reveal choreography.
rationale: Universal fade-up and stagger effects delay reading, cause distraction, break anchor navigation, and make all content claim equal theatrical importance.
applies_when: Most sections, cards, or lines of text animate on entry or scroll.
exceptions: A narrative scrollytelling experience may coordinate motion with the story when controls, reduced mode, and performance are designed explicitly.
implementation: Remove entry motion from content that can simply be present; retain state continuity and rare emphasis.
verification: The page feels complete and readable with animations disabled.
No content waits on intersection to become visible or accessible.
accessibility: Honor reduced motion, avoid vestibular patterns, and never hide semantic content until animation triggers.
responsive: Scroll thresholds work under short viewports, zoom, and orientation changes.
performance: Limit observers and concurrent animations; profile long frames and compositor memory.
browser_notes: Content remains available without IntersectionObserver or animation API support.

anti_pattern.why_ai_overuses: A reusable fade-up recipe adds obvious polish in demos, is simple to generate, and avoids reasoning about which changes actually need continuity.
detection: A shared reveal wrapper surrounds most sections.
Text starts hidden until intersection.
Stagger delay grows with list length.
Animations lack a named state transition.
appropriate_contexts: A small number of focal entrances, spatial state changes, or intentional narrative sequences.
inappropriate_contexts: Ordinary prose, long lists, forms, repeated cards, navigation, and content reached by anchors or search.
verification: Delete the generic reveal wrapper and justify any animation restored.
Test reduced motion, fast scroll, back navigation, anchors, and script failure.

title: Do not invent unsupported screenshot details
principle: Separate observed facts, measured inference, and creative assumptions; label or minimize any detail not supported by the reference or surrounding system.
rationale: Filling occluded regions with familiar generated patterns creates false fidelity and can misrepresent content, behavior, brand, or implementation constraints.
applies_when: Reconstructing from screenshots, partial designs, compressed assets, or incomplete references.
exceptions: A speculative reinterpretation may introduce new details when explicitly labeled and reviewed as design work, not reconstruction.
implementation: Maintain an evidence ledger for geometry, typography, content, assets, states, and behavior.
Use system-consistent neutral defaults for unavoidable gaps and request evidence when the assumption changes scope.
verification: Overlay and multi-viewport comparisons distinguish mismatch from unsupported invention.
Every material assumption is listed with confidence and can be replaced independently.
accessibility: Do not infer inaccessible interactions from a static visual; implement semantic, keyboard, and state behavior explicitly.
responsive: A single screenshot supports one observed viewport, not a complete breakpoint system.
performance: Do not reproduce accidental loading artifacts or oversized assets as intentional design.
browser_notes: Account for font rendering, scrollbar, form control, and capture-browser differences before changing design geometry.

anti_pattern.why_ai_overuses: Generative systems are optimized to complete patterns and often fill uncertainty with high-frequency landing-page structures instead of preserving unknowns.
detection: Unseen regions contain generic testimonials, logo clouds, metrics, or bento features.
Breakpoint behavior is asserted from one viewport.
Copy or icons appear that are absent from all references.
Implementation confidence is stated without measurements.
appropriate_contexts: Clearly labeled concept exploration or creative reinterpretation with user authorization.
inappropriate_contexts: Pixel reconstruction, forensic audits, parity work, migration verification, and evidence-backed reports.
verification: Classify each reconstructed decision as observed, inferred, or assumed.
Compare overlays at the observed viewport and avoid claiming fidelity outside it.

title: Do not fabricate proof, metrics, logos, or testimonials
principle: Publish claims and proof only when their source, scope, date, permission, and current accuracy are known; label placeholders and samples unmistakably.
rationale: Invented social proof and precision metrics deceive users, expose legal risk, and undermine the credibility the interface is trying to create.
applies_when: Showing customer names, quotes, ratings, usage counts, performance claims, certifications, awards, or security badges.
exceptions: Internal prototypes may use conspicuously fictional content that cannot be mistaken for production proof.
implementation: Attach provenance and review dates to proof content.
Use product evidence, transparent process, or remove the section when verified proof is unavailable.
verification: Each published claim resolves to an approved source and applicable scope.
Placeholder content cannot reach production through content or build pipelines.
accessibility: Proof is available as text and logos have appropriate alternatives without exaggerated accessible names.
responsive: Attribution and qualifiers are not hidden or truncated on small screens.
performance: Optimize approved logos and do not load a large carousel to simulate volume.
browser_notes: No browser-specific considerations beyond content and asset delivery.

anti_pattern.why_ai_overuses: Testimonials, logo clouds, and precise metrics are common landing-page slots, and language models can generate plausible names and numbers without access to provenance.
detection: Quotes have no source record or approval.
Customer logos are generic or unauthorized.
Metrics are round, precise, undated, or lack methodology.
Badges imply certification without a verifiable link.
appropriate_contexts: Verified, permissioned, current evidence with visible scope and attribution; unmistakably labeled internal sample data.
inappropriate_contexts: Production placeholders, speculative reconstructions, sales claims without methodology, and implied security certifications.
verification: Require source, owner, permission, scope, and review date for each claim.
Fail release when placeholder markers or unapproved proof assets remain.
